editableNewDialogue.text: I’m trying to plan out studying when the course feels kind of open-ended. How would you break it into manageable chunks?

A simple way is: (1) list the units/topics you’re responsible for, (2) set a weekly rotation (content review → practice problems → error review), and (3) use short “checkpoints” like a 15–25 question mixed quiz every few days to spot gaps.
How do I know if I’m actually improving and not just re-reading notes?

Use retrieval-based checks: do untimed problems first, then timed sets; track an error log (what went wrong and the fix); and revisit the same skill a few days later with new problems. If accuracy rises and your explanations get shorter/cleaner, that’s real progress.
Any good rule of thumb for how many practice problems per topic?

Enough to cover: a few easy (mechanics), several medium (mixed steps), and a couple hard (novel twist). Many people find ~15–30 well-chosen problems per subtopic works, but quality and review matter more than the raw count.
Random question: why do some old scientific constants get revised slightly over time?

Measurement improves. As instruments and methods get more precise, uncertainties shrink and recommended values shift. For example, Avogadro’s constant is now defined via the mole’s SI definition, but historically it was refined through better experiments and calibration standards.
I’m trying to make a plan on my own now, so I pulled up the AP Classroom topic list and started checking off what we already did, but I’m getting stuck deciding what order to hit the next units.

If you’re using the AP Classroom topics as your backbone, we can turn that list into a sequence and weekly checklist. Tell me which topics you’ve checked off so far and what your exam date window looks like.
Quick correction—I didn’t actually end up using *Chemistry: The Central Science*. I just found a copy that day, but it wasn’t the one I studied out of.
Got it—you *found* it, but you didn’t *use* it as your study textbook.
Yeah, exactly. I’m still basically looking for what to use now.
